_about: Build Nervous System (Stage 4, 'Land dark') client-side feature-flag manifest. Maps a flag name to { enabled, addedInPR, description }. Read once at init time by js/build/build-flags.js into an in-memory object — no localStorage, cookies, network, or server — so it behaves identically in the browser bundle, the Tauri desktop webview, and any future Tauri mobile build. Flag hygiene follows the promote-or-delete rule in docs/build-nervous-system.md: a flag left here for more than 3 merged PRs without being promoted (removed, code kept) or reverted (removed, code deleted) must be flagged in the 4th PR that touches this manifest. `addedInPR` records where each flag's clock started.

pluginPacks.description: Gen 40 domain-pack plugin architecture: assemble the active domain-pack source from self-contained plugin modules under js/packs/ via the pack registry instead of the inline DOMAIN_PACKS map. Behaviour-identical (same runtime pack objects); flip off to fall back to the legacy inline map.

conversationalPackBuilder.description: Gen 42 guided conversational pack builder: replace the blank-text-box pack authoring with a data-grounded question flow (js/agents/question-generator-agent.js), an on-device 'I don't know' resolver (js/agents/uncertainty-resolver-agent.js), a read-only peer-pack index (js/packs/local-pack-index.js), and an incremental pack builder (js/agents/pack-builder-agent.js). Backend (#89) + UI wiring (#91) both merged and tested; flipped on 2026-07-11 for live use in the Validate tab.

conversationalPackBuilderVoice.description: Gen 42 voice input for the conversational pack builder. The free-text answer path is fully implemented and identical to voice (voice is just transcribed text). No permissively-licensed on-device WASM/WebGPU STT model is vendored yet, so the mic is gated behind this flag with a TODO: pick a small Apache/MIT WASM speech model before promotion. When off, buildQuestionView hides the mic entirely and only the typed path shows.

metricContracts.description: Gen 44 Metric Contracts: an append-only version history for each Metric Studio definition (js/metrics/metric-contracts.js) plus a diff view (js/metrics/metric-contract-diff-view.js) and, in a later batch, a confirm-gate so any AI-agent-proposed change to a metric's formula/name/owner/filters renders as an exact diff and requires one explicit human click before it applies — nothing auto-applies, ever. Batch 1 shipped the pure versioned data model (MetricContractHistory/MetricContractRegistry + diffVersions). Batch 2 added the read-only diff-view/history-list content builders + DOM presenter (buildDiffViewContent/buildHistoryListContent/renderDiffView). Batch 3 (this PR) adds the confirm gate (js/metrics/metric-contract-confirm-gate.js) — proposeContractChange/approve/reject/renderConfirmGate — the ONLY path by which an AI-agent-proposed change can ever reach recordVersion(), gated on one explicit human click; still no caller wired into main.js, so this flag still currently gates nothing observable. It exists now so all batches in this generation share one flag from the start instead of adding a new one mid-stream.

metricStudio.description: OneCanvas Phase 1 (Part 5) Metric Studio: define a named metric in plain English tied to REAL columns of the loaded dataset, compute its formula against the in-browser DuckDB engine (js/metrics/metric-studio.js), store it in a local-only in-memory + export/import JSON registry, set its status (exploratory/reviewed/certified), and get merge/keep-both prompts on >90%-similar or same-formula duplicates. Ships dark: with the flag off (default) main.js never mounts the Metric Studio panel in the Validate tab and nothing renders.

trustStripProofDrawer.description: OneCanvas Phase 1 (Parts 3 & 4) Trust Strip + Proof Drawer: a persistent bar at the top of the Validate tab (js/trust/trust-strip.js) whose fields — freshness, metric certification counts, validation pass/fail, anomaly indicator, lineage availability, last update — all source from real computed data, and a slide-out Proof Drawer (js/trust/proof-drawer.js) that opens scoped to a clicked field or a Metric Studio metric, reusing the existing renderAttestationHTML()/renderReceiptHTML() for provenance. Ships dark: with the flag off (default) main.js never renders the strip and nothing shows.

aiReadinessGateBadge.description: AI Readiness Gate (batch 2 of 4) UI badge: a compact, INFORMATIONAL pass/fail pill (js/gate/readiness-gate-ui.js) shown below the SQL result in #sql-result-wrap. It composes the last real validation run (state.validationResults) via the pure batch-1 computeReadinessGate() into a single agent-consumability verdict — green 'Agent-ready', amber/red 'Not agent-ready' with the 0-100 score, and a click-to-expand reasons panel rendered from explainGateReasons(). It never re-runs validation, invents no new severity levels, and NEVER blocks or delays a query for a human — the hard block for agents is batch 3 (js/agents/*), not wired here. Only the SQL tab is wired in this batch; Python/R/Metric Studio and a real per-query metric-contract status are a documented follow-up. Flipped on 2026-07-12 (add-ai-readiness-gate-batch2-ui promoted): the informational badge now renders by default in the SQL tab; it remains purely informational and never blocks a human.

aiReadinessGateEnforcement.description: AI Readiness Gate (batch 3 of 4) AGENT hard-block enforcement: when on, DataGlow's own data-consuming agent modules (js/agents/question-generator-agent.js and js/agents/uncertainty-resolver-agent.js) refuse to produce output from a dataset/query result that the pure batch-1 computeReadinessGate() marks agentConsumable:false, returning a graceful refusal built from explainGateReasons() instead. It blocks ONLY js/agents/* automated output — never a human. Human-facing SQL/Python/R/Metric Studio workflows are entirely unaffected in both flag states. Enforcement is threaded through main.js's conversational pack builder ONLY when this flag is on (it passes the already-computed validation results as layerResults — no re-run of validation). Ships dark: with the flag off (its shipped default) the agents behave exactly as before this PR (no gate call, no refusal) and no human or agent path changes.

multiDialectSql.description: Polyglot Workbench (Batch A) multi-dialect SQL translation: a pure, standalone adapter (js/app-shell/sql-dialect-adapter.js) that transpiles SQL written in PostgreSQL, MySQL, BigQuery, Snowflake, or T-SQL into the DuckDB SQL the in-browser engine runs, via a small set of composable, literal-safe regex/string rules (no full parser). translateDialectSql(sql, dialect) is a pure string->string function (no DOM, no DuckDB import) and SUPPORTED_DIALECTS drives the picker. When on, the SQL tab shows a dialect-picker chip row above the editor and runs translateDialectSql(userSql, selectedDialect) before handing SQL to runQuery; the default selection is DuckDB (a byte-for-byte no-op passthrough). When off (its shipped default), no picker renders and the SQL tab runs the user's SQL exactly as before this PR. Batch B (an Object Space registry) is a separate, not-yet-started follow-up PR.

localAnalysisContract.description: Local Analysis Contract (js/validation/analysis-contract.js): before/alongside running a SQL query — the user's own, or one an AI assistant wrote — checks it offline against the REAL schema of the loaded dataset and flags schema hallucination (references to columns/tables that don't exist), aggregation mismatches (COUNT without DISTINCT across a JOIN; SUM of a rate-like column), and missing guard clauses (aggregate query never excludes test/deleted/refunded rows a matching column suggests it should). Flags-only, never rewrites or blocks a query — empowerment-constraint compliant. Renders as a dismissible card in #sql-result-wrap, above the result table. When off, the SQL tab behaves exactly as before this PR.

meetingScribe.description: Gen 43 meeting scribe: pure grounding/tagging logic (js/agents/meeting-scribe-agent.js, PR gen43-meeting-scribe-agent) plus a Meeting tab presenter (js/agents/meeting-scribe-ui.js, PR gen43-meeting-scribe-ui) where a person pastes or types a transcript to see pushback moments, data requests, and an action-item tracker enforcing the owner+dueDate+outcome rule. Still no audio capture or speech-to-text — those stay separate follow-up work. When this flag is off (its shipped default), renderTabBar() leaves the 'meeting' entry out of state.tabOrder entirely, so the tab is not just hidden but never added to the bar or reachable by any click path; #panel-meeting stays empty.

meetingDecisionLedger.description: Gen 43 meeting decision ledger: a permanent, append-only, chart-anchored local record of pushback moments, data requests, and action items surfaced by the meeting scribe (js/agents/meeting-decision-ledger.js pure logic, persisted via js/learning/memory-store.js's new meetingDecisionLedger IndexedDB object store, presented by js/agents/meeting-decision-ledger-ui.js). A separate flag from meetingScribe so this piece can ship dark independently of that flag's state. Nothing is saved automatically — the analyst must click 'Save this meeting to ledger' for anything to be written; browsing, filtering by chart/type, exporting to a local .json file, and clearing the ledger are all explicit, on-device actions with no network call anywhere in this feature. When this flag is off (its shipped default), #meeting-decision-ledger-body stays empty and the section is never mounted.

personalDataBom.description: Personal Data Bill of Materials (js/provenance/data-bom.js): a one-click, fully offline 'ingredient label' for the active dataset — composes the existing provenance attestation (js/provenance/provenance.js, unmodified) with a schema-shape signature, the column-distribution snapshot already computed for drift/validation (if one has been run), and — only when the user has explicitly loaded it — the local on-device AI model's exact id/quantization. Exports as signed-digest JSON or a printable HTML certificate via the same Export/Attestation card and download path the existing Export Attestation button uses. Same honest-labelling convention as provenance.js: a self-computed SHA-256 digest, never a claimed third-party notarization/signature. Empowerment-constraint compliant — this is a read-only export of what the user already has loaded; it never modifies data or triggers any automated action.

semanticDriftWatchdog.description: Turns the existing distribution_drift validation layer result into de-duplicated, non-spammy drift alerts on the Watch Folder panel, so an automatically re-validated file surfaces a NEW drift finding once instead of re-showing the same alert on every poll. Computes no new statistics of its own — js/ambient/drift-watchdog.js is a pure presentation + de-duplication layer over results validation.js's distribution_drift layer (and its forecast-based drift alerting) already produces on every run. A native (Tauri/Rust, OS-level file-event) trigger for the desktop shell is a deliberate, tracked follow-up — see docs/tech-debt-tracker.md — not part of this flag; today this only activates the browser-only Watch Folder poll path.

dataglowSidebarNav.description: Command Deck, Part 1: an alternate left-sidebar nav (#command-deck-sidebar, js/app-shell/command-deck-nav.js) that regroups the existing 13 tabs into 5 Trust-Tier Lifecycle Stages (Frame/Work/Trust/Generate/Tell) with collapsible stage headers, instead of the flat top tab bar. Pure reorganization of existing tabs -- zero new logic, zero new panels; every tab id is checked at test time against the real TAB_META in main.js so this can never silently drift out of sync with the app's actual 13 tools. Ships dark: with the flag off (default) the sidebar host stays hidden and the existing top tab bar remains the one and only nav, unchanged. Command Deck decision (direction / scope / naming) resolved by the agent per the user's 'build all, safely and smartly' instruction -- see docs/capability-map.md and AGENTS.md for the full stated rationale. Part 2 (command palette) and Part 3 (adaptive next-step rail) are follow-on batches, not included here.

semanticMetricsLayer.description: Semantic / Metrics Layer (js/validation/semantic-layer.js, Trust Passport Batch 1): a local, in-memory dictionary of human-authored metric definitions (name + canonical SQL expression + description + required columns + owner/createdAt provenance) plus a pattern-based comparator. Adds a FOURTH Local Analysis Contract finding class — metric_definition_mismatch — that flags when a query aliases/comments a result as a registered metric (e.g. SUM(amount) AS net_revenue) but its expression differs from the registered canonical definition (e.g. SUM(amount) - SUM(refund_amount)), naming the likely missing term. Not AI-powered: a plain registry + string/pattern comparator, no SQL AST, no model, no network. Flags-only, never rewrites/blocks/auto-authors — empowerment-constraint compliant. A minimal 'Define a metric' affordance in the SQL tab (js/validation/semantic-layer-ui.js) lets a human register a definition. Gated so the fourth check only runs when runAnalysisContract is passed a metric registry (main.js supplies one only when this flag is on); when off, the Contract behaves exactly as today (three finding classes only) and the Define-a-metric affordance is not shown. Registry is in-memory only, resets on reload — portable export/import is deferred to a later batch.

groupedNavigation.description: Groups the flat top-level tabs into 5 named modes — Explore (Problem Framer, Preflight), Validate & Trust (Clean, Validate, Diff), Analyze (SQL, Python, R), Visualize & Share (Visualize, Story), Automate (Digital Twin, Watch Folder) — rendered by js/app-shell/tab-groups.js's pure buildTabGroups(tabOrder)/groupForTab(tabId) reducer. This is a presentation-only change: no tab id, panel id, switchTab() call, drag-reorder handler, or flag gate for any individual tab is touched — renderTabBar() just renders the SAME state.tabOrder ids grouped under mode headers instead of in one flat row, and clicking a tab does exactly what it did before. When off (default), renderTabBar() renders the original flat single-row bar unchanged.

validateFocusMode.description: Declutters the Validate tab down to one primary action (Run All 20 Layers) by wrapping its secondary controls (context input, domain pack picker, level picker, export/import/receipt buttons) plus the AI Synthesis and Peer Review Mode cards in <details> disclosures, closed by default. js/app-shell/validate-focus.js's pure shouldExpandAdvanced({hasRunOnce, wasManuallyExpanded}) and createValidateFocusStore() decide open/closed state per dataset, in memory only — never persisted. A dataset's Advanced options auto-open the first time validation is run on it, or if the user manually opens any one of the three (they then track together). When off (default), applyValidateFocusMode() forces every one of these <details> open on every render — identical to the pre-PR fully-expanded layout, nothing removed or relabeled.

dataNutritionLabel.description: Trust Passport (Batch 2) Data Nutrition Label: a portable provenance manifest (js/provenance/data-nutrition-label.js) that knits the existing chain-of-custody trail, Assumption Ledger, and per-layer validation results into ONE self-describing summary — what was checked, what passed/failed, what transformations were recorded, any assumptions, whether the data is synthetic, and the chain of custody — that a recipient can inspect without running DATAGLOW. It is a summary/manifest only, NOT a certification or verification. Opt-in only: with the flag on, the Export card on the Visualize tab shows a 'Include Data Nutrition Label' checkbox; only when a human ticks it does the export append a human-readable label section (PDF/Excel) and download the machine-readable .json manifest alongside the existing export. When this flag is off (its shipped default), the checkbox is hidden and the export flow is byte-for-byte unchanged.

verifiableCheckSeal.description: Trust Passport (Batch 3) Verifiable Check Seal ("Proof-of-Clean"): seals a validation check result (e.g. a Local Analysis Contract run) into a portable Merkle-tree (SHA-256) commitment (js/provenance/verifiable-check-seal.js) that APPLIES the existing js/provenance/selective-disclosure-proof.js primitive — no new crypto. The seal lets a third party confirm 'a check with these parameters ran against data matching this exact SHA-256 fingerprint and produced this result' WITHOUT seeing the underlying data, and it can be attached additively to a batch-2 Data Nutrition Label's custodyChain (new custodyChain.seals array; no existing field changed). verifySeal genuinely re-checks: it re-folds every sealed claim to the committed Merkle root (detects any altered value) and, when data is supplied, re-fingerprints it so modified data fails to match — it is a real tamper check, not a 'verified' label. HONEST NAMING: it is NOT a zero-knowledge proof (parameters/result/fingerprints are cleartext), NOT a certification, NOT 'blockchain'; it does not attest the data is accurate or truthful, only that the check ran against the fingerprinted data and produced this result. Opt-in only: sealing is always an explicit human action (a 'Seal this result' button in the SQL tab's Analysis Contract flow), never automatic. When this flag is off (its shipped default), no seal UI renders and behaviour is exactly as before this PR.

syntheticDataPassport.description: Trust Passport (Batch 4, the finale) Governed Synthetic Data Passport: js/privacy/synthetic-data-passport.js COMPOSES batches 1-3 so a synthetic export never leaves 'naked'. buildSyntheticDataPassport() wraps batch 2's buildDataNutritionLabel (isSynthetic=true) with a `synthetic` block that HONESTLY describes how the data was generated and what privacy guarantee applies — a formal differential-privacy claim (mechanism + a specific ε) is asserted ONLY when the generation context actually establishes one (the Synthetic Twin and the DP aggregate export both do, via the Laplace mechanism), and the generator's own disclaimer is carried verbatim; where the method is heuristic / declares no DP the passport says plainly that no formal guarantee applies and the output may be re-identifiable. A DP budget is never upgraded into 'anonymized' / HIPAA Safe Harbor. sealSyntheticPassport() OPT-IN seals the exact generation parameters bound to a fingerprint of the synthetic OUTPUT via batch 3 and attaches it additively to the label custodyChain. Opt-in only: with the flag on, the Synthetic Twin card shows an 'Include Governance Passport' checkbox and, after generating, a 'Download Passport (.json)' / 'Seal + download' pair; only an explicit human click builds, seals, or downloads anything. When this flag is off (its shipped default), no passport UI renders and the synthetic-export flow is byte-for-byte unchanged.

objectSpaceRegistry.description: Polyglot Workbench (Batch B) Object Space registry: a shared, in-memory read model (js/app-shell/object-space.js — createObjectSpace/registerObject/listObjectSpace) that tracks the named objects living across the SQL (DuckDB), Python (Pyodide/pandas), and R (WebR) runtimes as one source of truth — each entry records name, originLanguage ('sql'|'python'|'r'), kind ('dataframe'|'model'|'scalar'), schema [{name,type}], rowCount, createdAt, and a provenance pointer into the existing chain-of-custody registry (no duplication of provenance.js logic). ADDITIVE and passive: it sits ALONGSIDE the existing per-language JSON round-trip bridges (dataglow.get_df / dataglow_get_df / FROM <table>) and does NOT replace their transfer mechanics, nor resolve cross-language references at query time (no working `FROM py.name` yet — a deliberate future batch). When on, the runtime wiring in js/app-shell/main.js additionally calls registerObject(...) after a SQL query result, and after each Python/R run for every dataset the bridge already exposed, and a small read-only 'Object Space' strip renders in the data sidebar listing live objects with an origin-language badge and row count/kind. When this flag is off (its shipped default), zero behavior changes anywhere — no registerObject call fires and no Object Space UI renders. Batch A (the multi-dialect SQL adapter) is a separate PR.

glowPathRail.description: Glow Path (Batch A) adaptive next-action rail: a single, honest 'what should I do next?' suggestion rail rendered into #glow-path-host, between the tab bar and the main area. The pure decision function (js/app-shell/glow-path.js — computeGlowPathState) composes fields the caller assembles from real state (dataset loaded/validated, validationSummary pass/warn/fail counts, an OPTIONAL real computeReadinessGate() output, and a repeat-query count) into one next action by a documented first-match-wins priority: (1) load a file, (2) run Validate, (3) an agent-readiness block explained from the gate's OWN failing layers (never fabricated; humans always still see everything), (4) review warnings, (5) save a repeated query (mid/high density only), (6) nothing actionable → renders nothing. The thin presenter (js/app-shell/glow-path-ui.js — buildGlowPathBadgeModel/renderGlowPath + an in-memory per-dataset createGlowPathDismissalStore) draws a dismissible rail, or NOTHING when there's no message. ADDITIVE alongside (not replacing) the tab bar, Command Deck sidebar, and tab groups. It NEVER re-runs validation or the gate — it reuses the output main.js already computed — and never blocks a human. densityLevel is an optional plain string (default 'low'); the proficiency-signal that would raise it is a separate parallel batch (Batch B) this module does not depend on. When this flag is off (its shipped default), main.js never calls the rail, #glow-path-host stays empty, and the app is byte-for-byte unchanged.
